questions.question: Why is 'use a rate limiter' not enough as a design answer?
options: Because the primitive changes burst handling, fairness, memory use, and smoothing behavior.
Because rate limiters only work for login systems.
Because every limiter requires a SQL database.
Because all limiter algorithms behave the same under load.
questions.explanation: The primitive affects the traffic shape the system allows or rejects.

questions.question: What is the main appeal of token bucket in many interviews?
options: It is simple, cheap on the hot path, and allows controlled bursts.
It guarantees exact fairness across every rolling second.
It removes all need for shared coordination.
It smooths output better than every queue-based approach.
questions.explanation: Token bucket is often a strong default because it handles bursts with little state.

questions.question: When is sliding window usually a better fit than token bucket?
options: When fairness over a rolling time interval matters more than minimal state cost.
When clocks do not exist on any node.
When you want to guarantee zero rejects.
When requests do not need any timestamps.
questions.explanation: Sliding window better approximates rolling fairness, but costs more state or computation.

questions.question: Why is leaky bucket often discussed for downstream protection?
options: It smooths outgoing work and can protect a fragile backend from sudden bursts.
It eliminates queueing entirely.
It is the only algorithm that works across regions.
It guarantees perfect abuse detection.
questions.explanation: Leaky bucket is about shaping the output rate, not just counting requests.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with choosing token bucket for a public API?
options: You get simple burst handling, but short adjacent windows may feel less strictly fair.
You lose the ability to reject requests.
You must store every timestamp forever.
You can no longer explain decisions to operators.
questions.explanation: Token bucket is efficient, but fairness is approximate around window boundaries.

questions.question: Which signal best shows that a chosen limiter primitive is mismatched to backend needs?
options: Backend saturation rises even while clients stay nominally within allowed average rate.
Markdown files become harder to read.
The limiter exposes a JSON API.
Global traffic volume remains flat.
questions.explanation: The primitive may be allowing unsafe burst or queue behavior for that backend.

questions.question: Why can sliding-window-style designs be more expensive at scale?
options: They often require more timestamps, buckets, or approximation logic per active key.
They can only run on one server.
They require a control plane for every request.
They cannot express rolling time windows.
questions.explanation: Rolling-window accuracy usually costs additional state or computation.

questions.question: What is the strongest first move when the interviewer asks which limiter primitive to use?
options: Clarify whether the system wants burst tolerance, fairness, or smoothed downstream throughput.
Choose leaky bucket immediately because it sounds advanced.
Assume all products need the same limiter.
Avoid discussing traffic shape.
questions.explanation: The right primitive depends on what behavior the business actually wants.
